overview.summary: A sophisticated cyber attack orchestrated by the Leviathan group targets transportation systems with the aim of disrupting operations. Utilizing a multi-stage approach, the attackers conduct extensive reconnaissance, develop resources, gain initial access via vulnerable remote services, execute malicious payloads, establish persistence, escalate privileges, evade defenses, access credentials, discover valuable information, move laterally, collect sensitive data, exfiltrate it over command and control channels, and finally impact critical operational functions.

Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Internet-facing assets, Public IP/Network Block, User Account, Command & Control Server, Logon Session, Target System Artifact
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Delivery Method, Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Response Metadata (Mechanism), User Account Authentication (Mechanism), Disable or Modify Tools, Mimikatz Tool, Network Service Scanning, Network Traffic Flow (Mechanism), Logon Session Creation (Mechanism), Exfiltration Over C2 Channel, Module Load (Mechanism)

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers engage in active scanning activities to map the network topology of the transportation systems, identifying potential entry points and vulnerabilities.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 522 Name: Malicious Hardware Component Replacement
stage.description: The adversaries acquire and setup command and control servers to facilitate their communication with compromised systems within the targeted transportation networks.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 555 Name: Remote Services with Stolen Credentials
stage.description: The attackers exploit weakly protected remote access services like VPNs or web portals to gain initial access to the transportation systems.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 160 Name: Exploit Script-Based APIs
stage.description: Once inside, the attackers execute malicious scripts to gain an initial foothold and establish a presence within the system.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 650 Name: Upload a Web Shell to a Web Server
stage.description: To maintain presence within the system, the attackers deploy a web shell on the command and control server.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 645 Name: Use of Captured Tickets (Pass The Ticket)
stage.description: Using compromised credentials, the attackers escalate their privileges within the system.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 578 Name: Disable Security Software
stage.description: The attackers disable or modify security tools to avoid detection and continue their operation undetected.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 226 Name: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: The attackers steal authentication materials from the operating system using tools like Mimikatz.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 309 Name: Network Topology Mapping
stage.description: The attackers map out the network to identify valuable targets and plan further lateral movement.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 324 Name: TCP (ISN) Sequence Predictability Probe
stage.description: The attackers move laterally across the network using legitimate remote services to expand their reach.

stage.name: Collection
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 277 Name: Data Interchange Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: The attackers gather sensitive data related to transportation operations from various repositories.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 24 Name: Filter Failure through Buffer Overflow
stage.description: The stolen data is transferred to attacker-controlled infrastructure over the established command and control channels.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 276 Name: Inter-component Protocol Manipulation
stage.description: The attackers disrupt critical functions of the transportation system by manipulating inter-process communications.

Scenario.summary: The Leviathan group employs a complex multi-stage attack strategy targeting transportation systems. They begin with extensive reconnaissance to identify vulnerabilities, followed by resource development to establish command and control infrastructure. Initial access is achieved through exploitation of weak remote services, leading to execution of malicious payloads and establishment of persistence. Privilege escalation occurs using compromised credentials, while defense evasion techniques are employed to avoid detection. The attackers then proceed to access credentials, discover valuable information, move laterally, collect sensitive data, and exfiltrate it back to their own infrastructure. Finally, they impact critical operations by disrupting key functionalities.
